skill.name: mock-strategy-guide
skill.description: Guides users on creating mock implementations for testing with traits, providing test doubles, and avoiding tight coupling to test infrastructure. Activates when users need to test code with external dependencies.
skill.summary: Guides users on creating mock implementations for testing with traits, providing test doubles, and a...
skill.author: EmilLindfors

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command execution, weak cryptography, and obfuscation indicators. Review found these are false positives caused by Markdown fenced Rust examples, frontmatter text, and descriptive wording; no executable shell, cryptographic code, network calls, or prompt injection instructions were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Fenced Rust Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution at Markdown code fence boundaries. The reviewed lines contain Rust testing examples inside SKILL.md, not executable shell commands or Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown code fence delimiters or adjacent Rust example blocks. No shell command invocation or Ruby backtick execution is present in the reviewed source.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography at the skill description. The reviewed line describes mock implementation guidance and contains no hash algorithm, cipher, or cryptographic operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged line is plain YAML frontmatter text. It does not reference MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic API.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Obfuscation Heuristic
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged bracket chains as possible obfuscation. The reviewed line is the opening YAML frontmatter marker and does not contain JavaScript, encoded payloads, or concealed instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged line is a standard Markdown frontmatter delimiter. No obfuscated JavaScript, chained bracket expression, or hidden payload was found.

content.value_statement: Rust services often depend on databases, HTTP clients, or other external systems that make tests slow and brittle. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward trait-based mocks, in-memory repositories, and focused fixtures.
actual_capabilities: Suggests trait-based mock implementations for Rust services with external dependencies.
Shows verification patterns for calls such as sent emails or saved records.
Provides examples for controlled success, not found, and failure behavior.
Demonstrates builder-style setup for readable mock test data.
Recommends in-memory repositories for tests that need real storage behavior without infrastructure.
Provides fixture helper patterns for reusable Rust test data.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a runnable Rust crate.
Examples may need adaptation to project-specific trait names, error types, and async runtimes.
It does not generate mocks from existing source files automatically.
It does not cover every mocking library or advanced concurrency case.

use_cases.title: Mock a Repository Trait
use_cases.description: Create a simple mock repository for unit tests that should not touch a real database.

use_cases.title: Verify Side Effects
use_cases.description: Track sent messages, saved records, or service calls so tests can assert behavior clearly.

use_cases.title: Plan Testable Architecture
use_cases.description: Identify dependency boundaries and propose traits that make business logic easier to test.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Mock
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for replacing an external dependency in a unit test.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a Rust mock implementation for this repository trait. Keep the test simple and avoid real infrastructure.

prompt_templates.title: Add Verification
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for proving a dependency was called with expected data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Rust mock that records calls so my test can verify the expected email, command, or saved entity.

prompt_templates.title: Model Failure Cases
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for testing error handling and edge cases.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show a Rust mock strategy that can return success, not found, and infrastructure errors for this service boundary.

prompt_templates.title: Review Test Architecture
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for improving a codebase with tightly coupled dependencies.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Rust testing approach and suggest trait boundaries, mock builders, and fixture helpers that improve maintainability.

output_examples.input: I need to test a user service without connecting to Postgres.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest a repository trait mock backed by an in-memory map, then show how to seed a user and assert the service result.

output_examples.input: My registration test must confirm that a welcome email was sent.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend a mock email service that records sent messages and exposes a helper for assertions.

output_examples.input: I want to test how my service handles database errors.
output_examples.output: The skill would propose configurable mock behavior for success, missing records, and database failures.

best_practices: Keep mocks close to the trait behavior that the unit under test actually needs.
Use builders or fixtures when repeated setup makes tests harder to read.
Prefer in-memory implementations when tests need realistic state changes without external services.
anti_patterns: Do not couple production code to test-only mock types.
Do not make mocks more complex than the behavior being verified.
Do not hide important failure paths behind mocks that always return success.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Rust tests?
faq.answer: No. It provides strategy and example patterns for writing tests, but it does not run commands.

faq.question: Can it work with async Rust traits?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include async trait implementations and Tokio-style test patterns.

faq.question: Does it require a mocking framework?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on hand-written mocks and in-memory implementations that fit trait-based designs.

faq.question: Can it help with hexagonal architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed around dependency boundaries such as repositories, services, and adapters.

faq.question: Will the examples compile unchanged?
faq.answer: Not always. They use illustrative names and must be adapted to the project types.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Yes. Review found only documentation examples and no executable command, network, or secret-handling behavior.
